senses.definition: Stops someone from breathing and causes them to lose consciousness or die.
examples: The thick smoke almost asphyxies anyone who enters the burning building.
Lack of fresh air asphyxies the trapped miners within minutes.

senses.definition: A serious condition in which a person cannot breathe and may become unconscious or die.
examples: The patient died from asphyxy caused by smoke inhalation.
Doctors treated the newborn quickly to prevent asphyxy during delivery.

senses.definition: A clear, savory jelly made from meat or fish stock, used to cover or hold pieces of meat, fish, or vegetables.
examples: The cold chicken was set in aspic and garnished with herbs.
They served vegetables in aspic as a starter at the formal dinner.

senses.definition: A non-standard or obsolete spelling of aspic, a savory jelly made from meat or fish stock.
examples: The old cookbook used the term aspick for what we now call aspic.
She misread the handwritten word aspick in the recipe.

senses.definition: Plural of aspick, an old or non-standard spelling of aspics.
examples: The menu listed several cold aspicks made from fish and poultry.
These handwritten recipes mention aspicks alongside pastries.

senses.definition: Plural of aspic, clear savory jellies made from meat or fish stock, used to hold or cover food.
examples: The buffet included several aspics decorated with vegetables.
She specializes in classic French aspics and terrines.

senses.definition: Having a short, sharp point at the tip, especially in descriptions of leaves or other plant parts.
examples: The aspiculate leaves help distinguish this species from related plants.
Botanists noted the aspiculate tips on the new specimens.

senses.definition: Having small pointed tips or projections, especially in technical descriptions of plants or other structures.
examples: Under the microscope, the aspiculous surface of the leaf became clear.
The aspiculous margin of the petal is a key identification feature.

senses.definition: Shield-shaped, especially in technical descriptions of biological structures.
examples: The aspidate scales protect the underside of the plant.
Certain fossils show aspidate plates along the body.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete term related to shield-shaped forms or to certain ferns, mainly used in scientific names.
examples: The genus name included the element aspide to show its shield-like sori.
In older texts, aspide appears in descriptions of fern groups.

senses.definition: A historical or technical plural form used in the scientific naming of certain shield ferns.
examples: The family once included several aspidia that are now placed in other genera.
In early classifications, aspidia were a common woodland group.

senses.definition: A technical taxonomic name formerly used for certain groups related to shield ferns.
examples: The ascomycete was once classified under Aspidiaria.
Older floras still list Aspidiaria alongside other fern genera.

senses.definition: A chemical compound obtained from certain ferns, formerly used to treat tapeworm infections.
examples: Aspidinol was once used as an anthelmintic drug.
Researchers isolated aspidinol from the rhizomes of the fern.

senses.definition: Resembling or related to ferns of the former genus Aspidium.
examples: The aspidioid fronds suggest it belongs to that fern group.
Several aspidioid species grow in shaded forests.

senses.definition: A genus of scale insects that attack plants, including important crop and ornamental species.
examples: Aspidiotus species can cause serious damage to citrus trees.
The infestation was identified as a member of the genus Aspidiotus.

senses.definition: A rarely used scientific term related to shield-like structures, mainly in specialist classifications.
examples: The aspidiske was described in an early zoological monograph.
Only a few researchers still use the word aspidiske.

senses.definition: A tough indoor plant with large, pointed leaves, often grown for its foliage.
examples: An aspidistra stood in a pot by the front door.
They chose an aspidistra because it can survive in low light.

senses.definition: Plural of aspidistra, tough indoor plants with large pointed leaves.
examples: The hallway was lined with dusty aspidistras.
She keeps several aspidistras in the shady living room.

senses.definition: A former genus name for certain shield ferns, now usually included in other genera.
examples: Many species once placed in Aspidium are now reclassified.
The old literature refers to Aspidium in descriptions of woodland ferns.

senses.definition: A former taxonomic group of gastropod molluscs characterized by certain gill structures.
examples: Aspidobranchia was once treated as a separate order of gastropods.
Modern classifications rarely use the name Aspidobranchia.

senses.definition: Belonging to or having the characteristics of the group Aspidobranchia.
examples: The species shows typical aspidobranchiata features.
Aspidobranchiata forms were common in ancient seas.

senses.definition: Having the type of gills or characteristics seen in the former group Aspidobranchia.
examples: Aspidobranchiate molluscs possess a particular gill arrangement.
The fossil shows aspidobranchiate traits in its shell structure.

senses.definition: A former name for a group of fossil armored fishes with shield-like heads.
examples: Aspidocephali are known from Devonian rock formations.
The class Aspidocephali includes several extinct fish orders.

senses.definition: A former order or group of sea cucumbers with shield-like tentacles.
examples: Aspidochirota are usually large sea cucumbers found on the sea floor.
The order Aspidochirota is no longer used in some modern systems.

senses.definition: A little-used taxonomic name for a group of ray-finned fishes with shield-like features.
examples: Aspidoganoidei are mentioned in older ichthyology texts.
No living species are normally assigned to Aspidoganoidei today.

senses.definition: A form of divination in which people try to tell the future using a shield or its symbols.
examples: In folklore, aspidomancy was practiced by warriors before battle.
The book describes ancient methods of aspidomancy and other forms of magic.

senses.definition: A genus of South American trees whose bark has been used in medicine.
examples: Aspidosperma trees are native to tropical America.
Some Aspidosperma species were used as traditional remedies.

senses.definition: An alkaloid found in the bark of Aspidosperma trees, once used as a drug.
examples: Aspidospermine was investigated for its effects on the respiratory system.
Chemists isolated aspidospermine from the dried bark.

senses.definition: Without spines or sharp points.
examples: Unlike related species, this cactus is almost aspine.
The aspine leaves make the plant safe for children.

senses.definition: Plural of aspine, referring to organisms or structures without spines.
examples: These aspines are often confused with their spiny relatives.
In the study, aspines showed fewer injuries to grazing animals.

senses.definition: A very rare spelling related to aspic or a similar dish in historical texts.
examples: The handwritten menu listed an aspiquee among the cold dishes.
She could not find any modern recipe for aspiquee.

senses.definition: A person who strongly wants a particular job, position, or level of success.
examples: There were many aspirants for the role of team leader.
As a young political aspirant, she worked long hours for the party.

senses.definition: Trying to achieve a higher position, status, or level of success.
examples: The company offers training for aspirant managers.
He attended a workshop for aspirant filmmakers.

senses.definition: People who strongly want a particular job, position, or level of success.
examples: Dozens of aspirants applied for the scholarship.
The party interviewed several aspirants for the candidacy.

senses.definition: In linguistics, a consonant sound that is pronounced with a strong breath, such as an aspirated stop.
examples: The grammarian listed the Greek aspirata separately.
In some languages, the aspirata contrasts with an unaspirated sound.

senses.definition: Plural form of a technical term for aspirated consonant sounds in classical languages.
examples: The teacher explained how the aspiratae were pronounced in Latin.
The chart shows the Greek aspiratae alongside other stops.

senses.definition: To pronounce a sound with a strong breath of air.
examples: In English, we usually aspirate the p in 'pin'.
Some learners find it hard to aspirate certain consonants.

senses.definition: To remove liquid or gas from a body or space by suction, especially in medicine.
examples: The nurse will aspirate the fluid from the joint.
Doctors aspirate blood using a special syringe.

senses.definition: To accidentally draw food, liquid, or another substance into the lungs while breathing.
examples: Young children can aspirate small objects while playing.
He aspirated some water and started coughing badly.

senses.definition: A sound, such as the English h, that is pronounced with a strong breath of air.
examples: Some languages do not use the h aspirate at all.
The chart shows which consonants are aspirates.

senses.definition: Pronounced with a strong breath of air.
examples: In some languages, aspirated and unaspirated stops change the meaning.
The teacher marked the aspirated consonants in the word list.

senses.definition: Drawn out by suction, especially from the body in medical procedures.
examples: The aspirated fluid was sent to the lab for testing.
Aspirated blood should be handled carefully.

senses.definition: Third person singular of aspirate: pronounces a sound with a strong breath of air.
examples: She aspirates the initial consonant more than native speakers do.
He aspirates the p in 'spin', which sounds unusual.

senses.definition: Plural of aspirate, sounds that are pronounced with a strong breath of air.
examples: In this language, the aspirates contrast with plain stops.
The table lists all the aspirates in the phonetic system.

senses.definition: Present participle of aspirate: pronouncing a sound with a strong breath of air.
examples: Learners often practice aspirating consonants in minimal pairs.
He is aspirating the t too strongly in this word.

senses.definition: Present participle of aspirate: removing liquid or gas by suction, or accidentally drawing something into the lungs.
examples: The doctor is aspirating fluid from the patient’s chest.
Be careful to avoid aspirating food while lying down.

senses.definition: A strong hope or wish to achieve something, especially something important in your life or career.
examples: Her main aspiration is to become a doctor.
The program supports the aspirations of young entrepreneurs.

senses.definition: The act of pronouncing a sound with a strong breath of air.
examples: Aspiration of the initial consonant can change the meaning in this language.
In phonetics, aspiration is marked with a special symbol.

senses.definition: The act of removing liquid or gas from the body by suction, or of breathing something into the lungs.
examples: Aspiration of the fluid relieved the patient’s pain.
The child developed pneumonia after aspiration of food.

senses.definition: Showing a strong desire to achieve a higher social position, standard of living, or level of success.
examples: They moved to an aspirational neighborhood in the suburbs.
The brand targets aspirational middle-class consumers.

senses.definition: Serving as something people dream of having or becoming, rather than something that is easily reached.
examples: The advertisement presents an aspirational image of family life.
Luxury cars are often sold as aspirational products.

senses.definition: In a way that expresses a strong desire to achieve more success, status, or a better standard of living.
examples: The campaign is aspirationally framed to appeal to young professionals.
He speaks aspirationally about his future career.

senses.definition: Strong hopes or wishes to achieve something, especially important goals in life or work.
examples: Her parents fully support her academic aspirations.
The survey asked students about their career aspirations.

senses.definition: A medical device that removes liquid or gas from the body by suction.
examples: The nurse used an aspirator to clear mucus from the patient’s airway.
Portable aspirators are essential in ambulances.

senses.definition: A device that uses suction to collect small insects or other particles for study.
examples: The entomologist carried an aspirator to collect tiny beetles.
Insect aspirators help researchers sample flying insects safely.

senses.definition: Plural of aspirator, devices that remove liquids or gases by suction, often in medical or scientific settings.
examples: Modern hospitals rely on several types of aspirators.
The lab stores the aspirators in a locked cabinet.

senses.definition: Related to aspiration, especially the removal of fluid or the drawing in of material by suction.
examples: The patient required aspiratory support after surgery.
Aspiratory techniques are discussed in the medical handbook.

senses.definition: To strongly hope or plan to achieve something, especially an important job, position, or level.
examples: Many young people aspire to study abroad.
She aspires to become a successful architect.

senses.definition: Past tense of aspire: strongly hoped or planned to achieve something.
examples: As a child, he aspired to be a pilot.
They aspired to a better life in the city.

senses.definition: A rare or informal word for a person who aspires to something.
examples: Each aspiree wrote an essay describing their goals.
The workshop was full of creative aspirees.

senses.definition: Someone who aspires to reach a particular goal or position.
examples: Every aspirer must be ready to face setbacks.
Political aspirers attended the leadership camp.
